telegram_footer: 10 items surfaced | 30/30 current front-page stories processed | history + run artifact updated.
summary: Ten comment-endorsed tools and projects from a verified full crawl of the current Hacker News front page.

items.name: FreeInk
items.summary: An open ecosystem for hackable, lightweight e-readers and custom firmware.
items.why_included: A commenter called the project “dangerously cool,” making it a strong open-hardware reading pick.
items.evidence: An HN commenter said FreeInk is “already dangerously cool” and could become far more consequential with Kindle support.

items.name: Witch Reader
items.summary: An e-reader firmware with enhanced book rendering and CrossPoint-compatible features.
items.why_included: It had unusually concrete, comparative praise from someone who tried many alternatives.
items.evidence: A commenter called Witch Reader their favorite, praising its rendering and saying they keep coming back to it.

items.name: AALU
items.summary: An e-reader firmware focused on grouping book series together visually.
items.why_included: A focused recommendation for readers who want better library organization on small e-ink devices.
items.evidence: A commenter recommended AALU as worth a look for displaying all books in a series together.

items.name: inx
items.summary: A Rust-oriented e-reader firmware notable for annotation support.
items.why_included: It is a niche but concrete firmware option with a distinctive capability absent from peers.
items.evidence: A commenter called inx their favorite and said it was the only option they knew with annotation support, while noting occasional crashes.

items.name: Justif
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Justif – Knuth-Plass justification and microtypography for the web
items.summary: A web typography library bringing Knuth-Plass line breaking and microtypography to CSS layouts.
items.why_included: Real-world testing reported typeset-quality columns, small payload size, and fast long-document layout.
items.evidence: After testing a 5k-word, three-column page, a commenter said Justif’s output “looks like typesetting rather than CSS.”

items.name: Laguna S 2.1
items.summary: An open-weight 118B-parameter MoE coding model designed for practical local deployment.
items.why_included: Several users reported capable local coding results, with an important configuration caveat for thinking mode.
items.evidence: A commenter reported markedly better code quality once thinking was enabled and another described the model as “great for coding.”

items.name: llm-compressor
items.summary: A model-compression tool that can quantize models too large to fit in memory.
items.why_included: It solves a practical local-model deployment constraint and came with specific first-hand usage guidance.
items.evidence: A commenter said they use llm-compressor’s sequential pipeline to quantize models that do not fit in memory.

items.name: PCjs Machines
items.summary: Browser-based emulations of vintage PCs, software, and operating systems.
items.why_included: A first-hand test demonstrated a complete browser-to-real-hardware Windows 3.1 development loop.
items.evidence: A commenter created a Visual Basic executable in PCjs, moved it to real 1992 hardware, and reported it ran without issue.

items.name: Filmot
items.source_story_title: Gemini 3.6 Flash, 3.5 Flash-Lite, and 3.5 Flash Cyber
items.summary: A search engine for finding YouTube videos through their transcripts.
items.why_included: It is a durable, directly useful discovery tool for recovering lectures and videos from partial memories.
items.evidence: A commenter said they have used Filmot for years to find lectures when they remember only fragments.

items.name: Late.sh
items.source_story_title: Late.sh – a command-line Clubhouse for computer people
items.summary: A command-line social space for live computer-oriented conversation, music, games, and bots.
items.why_included: It drew unambiguous enthusiasm for both its unusual interaction model and polished presentation.
items.evidence: HN commenters called Late.sh “amazing” and “fantastic,” highlighting its Icecast, IRC, 2048, and LLM features.
